sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 37-year-old male, presents with a 2-week history of flank pain radiating to the groin, which is exacerbated by fluid intake and physical activity.
The pain is described as a 1-4 out of 10, with periods of increased severity.
He reports no nausea, vomiting, or fever.
He has a history of passing kidney stones in the past, including surgical interventions.
Past medical history: The patient has a family history of kidney stones, with multiple family members having experienced the condition.
He has a history of passing kidney stones himself, including self-passed stones and surgical interventions.
Review of systems: The patient denies any symptoms of infection, such as fever or chills.
Current medications: I prescribed ibuprofen for pain management.
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's blood pressure is elevated, likely due to pain.
Other vital signs are not mentioned.
Physical exam: The patient's abdomen is tender to palpation on the right side, specifically in the CVA area, consistent with a kidney stone.
There is no tenderness to palpation of the abdomen.
Test Results: A CT scan showed a 0.5 cm stone located on the proximal right ureter.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a kidney stone measuring 0.5 cm in size, located on the proximal right ureter, with no evidence of high flow nephrosis.
Problem list: Kidney stone (active); history of kidney stones (past); elevated blood pressure (status unknown).
Plan:
I will continue the patient on ibuprofen for pain management and prescribe tramadol for breakthrough pain.
I recommend continued use of a strainer when urinating to attempt to catch the stone.
I will order a BMP, urinalysis, and urine culture to evaluate for infection.
I will see the patient again in 1-2 weeks to reassess symptoms and consider further treatment, including lithotripsy.
Follow-up: I will see the patient again in 1-2 weeks to reassess symptoms and consider further treatment.
